text: This is a laparoscopic removal of a giant right adrenal hemangioma. A sixty seven year old male with mild hypertension had elevated liver function tests on routine labs. An abdominal ultrasound revealed a large right sided adrenal mass. A subsequent CT scan showed a heterogeneous thirteen centimeter adrenal mass with areas of central necrosis and a localized mass effect. CT guided biopsy showed hematoma cortical adrenal cells but no evidence of malignancy. He denied headaches, palpitations, weight gain or loss, abdominal pain, or muscle weakness. An extensive endocrine evaluation was negative for a functioning adrenal tumor. However, he was incidentally found to have hypothyroidism.

The patient is positioned in the left lateral decubitus position for a laparoscopic right adrenalectomy. The surgeon enters the abdomen in the right upper quadrant using a 5mm optically guided trocar. Adhesions between the left lobe of the liver and the omentum are dissected. Extensive adhesions between the adrenal tumor and the liver capsule are carefully separated using blunt dissection and bipolar electrocautery, avoiding direct manipulation of the tumor. The retroperitoneal space is accessed, and a curved bipolar electrocautery device is used to expose the blood supply to the adrenal tumor, including the adrenal vein and inferior vena cava.

The surgeon frees the large adrenal mass from its inferior peritoneal attachments, avoiding direct grasping of the tumor itself. Instead, the pericapsular fat or attached peritoneum is manipulated. The left lobe of the liver is held out of the surgical field using a self-retaining liver retractor, providing exposure. The vascular supply to the adrenal mass is addressed using a combination of blunt dissection, suction, sharp dissection, and electrocautery. The liver retractor is visible on the right, while a blunt grasper pulls the adrenal tumor to the left.

The surgeon dissects around the medial portion of the adrenal tumor, identifying and ligating several feeding vessels from the direction of the vena cava using bipolar cautery. The inferior margin of the adrenal mass is separated from the adjacent kidney, revealing a clear plane between the structures. A dominant vessel, presumed to be the adrenal vein, is clipped and divided. The adrenal tumor is visible on the right side of the screen as the lateral peritoneal attachments and superior attachments to the liver capsule are transected.

The surgeon continues dissection to release the adrenal tumor from its attachments, fully mobilizing it. The normal adrenal gland is excised and placed in a 10mm endo-catch bag, while the primary lesion is placed in a 15mm endo bag. A 12cm upper midline incision is made, protected with a wound protector, and the large, firm lesion is extracted through the incision.

The final pathology for the right adrenal mass was a cavernous hemangioma of adrenal gland with large hematoma. It had an intact capsule and the dimensions were thirteen point five by twelve by eight centimeters and it weighed seven hundred and seventy five grams. The remaining right adrenal gland showed unremarkable adrenal gland tissue.

The patient did well and was discharged on post-operative day four. At his one-month follow-up, he was pain-free and no longer taking steroids. Laparoscopic adrenalectomy has long been described as a safe way to manage adrenal tumors. The average size of an adrenal mass removed laparoscopically is about seven centimeters. In this case, we removed a thirteen centimeter adrenal hemangioma. Giant adrenal tumors can safely be removed laparoscopically.
